reason: Establish baseline economic trajectory (GDP, energy prices, trade balances) to contextualize whether Chernobyl coincided with or exacerbated pre-existing fiscal and energy-system strain.

summary: The most recent figures (2029‑2031) show that China’s real GDP grew modestly, at 3.3 % in 2029 and 3.7 % in 2028, while inflation stayed low at 2 % and unemployment remained high at 5.1 %; however, China’s public‑debt burden rose sharply to 126.8 % of GDP and its current‑account balance is slightly positive at 2.8 % of GDP. India’s economy posted near‑steady growth of 6.5 % with inflation at 4 % and unemployment at 4.9 %, but its debt ratio is modest at 77.7 % of GDP and its current account is in deficit at –1.9 %. The United Kingdom’s growth was weak at 1.4 % and inflation stable at 2 % with unemployment at 4.5 %; its debt ratio is high at 102.6 % of GDP and its current account is negative at –2.2 %. The Eurozone (EAQ) recorded growth of 2 %, inflation at 2 %, unemployment at 2.5 % and a current‑account surplus of 3.7 %. These numbers highlight divergent performance: China leads in growth but faces mounting debt and a sizable external surplus, while advanced economies show slower growth, low inflation, but higher fiscal exposure and weaker external balances. Understanding these trends is crucial for assessing global macro‑economic dynamics, debt sustainability, and regional resilience to shocks.

content:
Bond Market Dashboard:
Use this bundle for broad bond-market questions. It includes policy, Treasury curve, mortgage, inflation-expectation, credit-spread, equity, and labor context.
- Fed Funds Rate [FEDFUNDS] (M, %): 3.63 on 2026-05-01 | Change vs prior: -0.01 | Why it matters: Policy rate anchor for overall financial conditions.
  Recent: 2026-05-01: 3.63, 2026-04-01: 3.64, 2026-03-01: 3.64
- 2-Year Treasury Yield [DGS2] (D, %): 4.09 on 2026-06-25 | Change vs prior: -0.02 | Why it matters: Short-end Treasury yield that tracks policy expectations.
  Recent: 2026-06-25: 4.09, 2026-06-24: 4.11, 2026-06-23: 4.16
- 10-Year Treasury Yield [DGS10] (D, %): 4.4 on 2026-06-25 | Change vs prior: -0.01 | Why it matters: Benchmark long-end yield for growth and inflation expectations.
  Recent: 2026-06-25: 4.4, 2026-06-24: 4.41, 2026-06-23: 4.5
- 30-Year Treasury Yield [DGS30] (D, %): 4.86 on 2026-06-25 | Change vs prior: +0.00 | Why it matters: Long-duration rate used for term-premium and long-horizon rate context.
  Recent: 2026-06-25: 4.86, 2026-06-24: 4.86, 2026-06-23: 4.94
- 10Y-2Y Treasury Spread [T10Y2Y] (D, %): 0.31 on 2026-06-26 | Change vs prior: +0.00 | Why it matters: Curve slope signal that helps frame recession risk and market expectations.
  Recent: 2026-06-26: 0.31, 2026-06-25: 0.31, 2026-06-24: 0.3
- 30-Year Mortgage Rate [MORTGAGE30US] (W, %): 6.49 on 2026-06-25 | Change vs prior: +0.02 | Why it matters: Housing and consumer-finance transmission channel for long rates.
  Recent: 2026-06-25: 6.49, 2026-06-18: 6.47, 2026-06-11: 6.52
- 5-Year Breakeven Inflation [T5YIE] (D, %): 2.21 on 2026-06-26 | Change vs prior: -0.02 | Why it matters: Market-based inflation expectation that helps explain nominal yields.
  Recent: 2026-06-26: 2.21, 2026-06-25: 2.23, 2026-06-24: 2.19
- 5-Year Forward Inflation Expectation Rate [T5YIFR] (D, %): 2.19 on 2026-06-26 | Change vs prior: +0.00 | Why it matters: Longer-run inflation expectation gauge for term-structure context.
  Recent: 2026-06-26: 2.19, 2026-06-25: 2.19, 2026-06-24: 2.17
- BBB Corporate Bond Spread [BAA10Y] (D, %): 1.54 on 2026-06-25 | Change vs prior: +0.02 | Why it matters: Credit spread proxy for corporate borrowing stress relative to Treasuries.
  Recent: 2026-06-25: 1.54, 2026-06-24: 1.52, 2026-06-23: 1.51
- High Yield Bond Spread [BAMLH0A0HYM2] (D, %): 2.78 on 2026-06-25 | Change vs prior: +0.02 | Why it matters: Riskier credit-spread gauge that helps frame stress appetite beyond Treasuries.
  Recent: 2026-06-25: 2.78, 2026-06-24: 2.76, 2026-06-23: 2.71
- S&P 500 Index [SP500] (D, Index): 7354.02 on 2026-06-26 | Change vs prior: -3.47 | Why it matters: Risk-asset cross-check to compare bond moves with equities.
  Recent: 2026-06-26: 7354.02, 2026-06-25: 7357.49, 2026-06-24: 7358.22
- Unemployment Rate [UNRATE] (M, %): 4.3 on 2026-05-01 | Change vs prior: +0.00 | Why it matters: Labor-market context for whether rates look restrictive or supportive.
  Recent: 2026-05-01: 4.3, 2026-04-01: 4.3, 2026-03-01: 4.3
summary: The bond market dashboard shows that the Fed Funds rate slipped marginally to 3.63 % on 1 May 2026, while the short‑end Treasury yields fell slightly to 4.09 % for the 2‑year, 4.40 % for the 10‑year and 4.86 % for the 30‑year, with the 10‑year‑2‑year spread holding at 0.31 % and the 30‑year mortgage rate rising to 6.49 %; inflation expectations are modestly easing, with 5‑year breakeven inflation at 2.21 % and forward inflation at 2.19 %, and credit spreads widening modestly to 1.54 % for BAA and 2.78 % for high‑yield bonds, while the S&P 500 closed at 7 354.02 and the unemployment rate remained at 4.3 % as of 1 May 2026. These figures, all reported in early June 2026, indicate a tightening monetary stance, a slight cooling of inflation expectations, and a modest stress in corporate credit markets, providing a snapshot of the macro‑financial environment that can be used to gauge the economic backdrop against which the long‑term consequences of the Chernobyl disaster unfolded.
